content.text: The story of Bhakta Prahlada. Prahlada, the son of the demon king Hiranyakashipu, was a supreme devotee of Lord Vishnu. Despite his father's fierce attempts to kill him and torture him, Prahlada's faith remained unshaken. Lord Vishnu emerged as Narasimha (the half-man half-lion avatar) to protect Prahlada, destroy the tyrant demon king, and prove that true devotion is always protected by the Supreme Lord.

The story of Bhakta Dhruva. The young prince Dhruva, rejected by his stepmother, set out into the forest at the age of five to seek Lord Vishnu. Guided by Sage Narada, he performed intense penance (tapasya) meditating on 'Om Namo Bhagavaty Vasudevaya'. Impressed by his unwavering focus and purity, Lord Vishnu appeared before him, granting him eternal peace and the honor of becoming the pole star (Dhruva Tara).

The story of Sudama Vipra. Sudama, a poor brahmana, was a childhood friend of Lord Krishna. Living in extreme poverty, his wife urged him to visit Krishna in Dwaraka for help. Sudama went empty-handed except for a handful of flattened rice (poha) wrapped in a torn cloth. Krishna welcomed Sudama with royal honors, washed his feet, and ate the rice with great delight. Sudama asked for nothing out of devotion, but upon returning home, found his humble hut transformed into a magnificent golden palace.

The story of Gajendra Moksha. Gajendra, the king of elephants, was caught by a mighty crocodile in a lake. They fought for a thousand years, and as Gajendra grew weak and realized his own strength was failing, he surrendered completely to the Lord. Offering a single lotus flower to Lord Vishnu, Gajendra prayed for liberation. Lord Vishnu arrived on Garuda, severed the crocodile's head with his Sudarshana Chakra, and granted Gajendra Moksha (liberation).
